text: The video shows a four-centimeter umbilical hernia located at the umbilicus, with a significant amount of omentum visibly adhered to the hernia sac.
The surgeon observes the bowel component of the hernia, noting no dilated bowel loops. The omentum is visible and being gently pushed to reduce the hernia, despite its incarceration.
The surgeon carefully reduces the contents of the hernia sac, gradually pushing the adhered omentum back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a small sponge in the left hand to apply pressure on the hernia site, performing gentle blunt dissection to separate adhered omentum within the umbilical hernia.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the omentum, facilitating the release of the hernia neck and allowing the remaining fat to protrude.

The surgeon performs slow dissection to separate the entire hernia sac, ensuring that no components of the omentum are left inside. The natural fat of the peritoneum is visible and left intact, while external contents are carefully removed from the sac.

The surgeon performs dissection to separate adhered tissue using either bipolar forceps or harmonic scalpel. The laparoscopic repair of the ventral hernia is underway, with attention to ensuring the mesh size is adequate, specifically twelve centimeters plus the defect size.

The surgeon positions a polyurethane dual mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring the center of the mesh aligns with the center of the defect. The mesh features Gore-Tex Tads at its four corners. The white side of the mesh is oriented towards the abdominal wall, while the blue side faces the bowel. All omentum is visibly separated, and the hernia sac contents are completely reduced.
The surgeon displays the dual-colored mesh, with the white polypropylene component facing the abdominal wall and the blue polyurethane component facing the bowel, designed to reduce adhesion.

The surgeon stretches the mesh across the hernia defect, ensuring all four corners are evenly positioned without any corrugation, using a synthetic coated mesh preferred for its stability and reduced allergic reactions.

The surgeon uses a suture that emerges from the same skin prick but penetrates the rectus at a different level, allowing the knot to be tied subcutaneously. The ProTac device, a titanium implant, is then used to secure the mesh by performing both outer and inner crowning.

The surgeon uses the ProTac device to apply both outer and inner crowning circles to stabilize the mesh. The left hand is used to oppose the mesh from above, ensuring the abdominal wall is perpendicular to the ProTac tip for deeper penetration, without reaching the skin.

The surgeon ensures the ProTac is not fired at the defect level, avoiding the inferior epigastric vessel to prevent bleeding. The two medial umbilical ligaments are visible, and the ProTac is applied on either side without extending too laterally to avoid the vessel.

The surgeon positions the ProTac device to perform the inner crowning, ensuring that the tacks are placed on the margin of the defect rather than directly over it. Care is taken to avoid firing the tacks over the thin skin at the defect site to prevent skin pricking and reduce the risk of infection.
The surgeon completes firing the ProTac implants around the mesh during the laparoscopic repair of an umbilical hernia, ensuring the mesh is securely fixed without protruding through the skin.
